module_microarchitecture.name: sram_mbist_demo_controller
module_microarchitecture.implementation_style: Simple synchronous MMIO-style controller around a 64x32 single-port SRAM, with explicit memory-intent signals and lightweight software-visible control/status registers.
module_microarchitecture.control_strategy: Use a single clocked process to decode register writes and reads, update control/status state, generate the SRAM command signals, and latch BIST/IRQ status. Keep the BIST path simple by OR-ing the direct bist_start input with the BIST_CONTROL.START software request bit into a single bist_req intent signal. Do not implement a complex multi-state BIST engine; preserve only the explicit request, running, done, fail, and last-fail-address collateral required by the spec.
module_microarchitecture.state_strategy: Store register-visible state in a small set of synchronous flops: CONTROL, MEM_ADDR, MEM_WDATA, MEM_RDATA, MEM_CONTROL pulse state, BIST_CONTROL pulse state, BIST_STATUS bits, IRQ_STATUS bits, ready, bist_done, bist_fail, irq, bist_done_latched, bist_fail_latched, last_fail_addr, and any minimal internal busy/command bookkeeping needed to drive the single-port SRAM. Reset synchronously clears all of these to zero or idle-safe values. Upper bits of wr_addr and rd_addr are ignored for SRAM access and only the lower 6 bits are used for mem_addr.
module_microarchitecture.output_strategy: Drive rd_data from the selected MMIO register readback path, returning the current register value for the addressed location and the software-visible memory read data for MEM_RDATA. Drive ready as an idle/available indicator consistent with the simple register/memory access model. Drive bist_done and bist_fail from latched BIST status, and drive irq only when IRQ_ENABLE is set and either latched done or fail status is asserted. Drive mem_csb, mem_web, mem_addr, and mem_din directly from the current firmware memory access intent so synthesis and DFT tools can recognize the SRAM-like structure.
ownership_notes: Owns rd_data, ready, bist_done, bist_fail, irq, mem_csb, mem_web, mem_addr, mem_din, bist_req, irq_enable, bist_done_latched, bist_fail_latched, and last_fail_addr.
Receives clk, reset_n, wr_en, wr_addr, wr_data, rd_en, rd_addr, bist_start, and mem_dout.
Must preserve the software-visible register map exactly as specified.
Must not introduce hidden buses, new ports, or extra hierarchy.

module_microarchitecture.name: demo_sram_32x64
module_microarchitecture.implementation_style: Macro-like single-port SRAM abstraction intended for OpenRAM binding when collateral is available.
module_microarchitecture.control_strategy: Model a synchronous, chip-selected single-port memory with active-low chip select and active-low write enable. Treat csb deasserted as no access. Use the provided clk, csb, web, addr, and din interface only.
module_microarchitecture.state_strategy: If implemented as a modeled memory view, represent the 64x32 contents as a simple synchronous storage array with macro-like behavior. Do not add reset semantics. Keep the interface and naming aligned with OpenRAM expectations.
module_microarchitecture.output_strategy: Drive dout as the synchronous read data result of the addressed location under the selected single-port latency model. Maintain memory-like visibility for downstream memory-test and synthesis flows.
ownership_notes: Receives clk, csb, web, addr, and din.
No reset port exists and no resettable behavior should be inferred.
The canonical macro name must remain demo_sram_32x64.

module_microarchitecture.name: demo_sram_32x64_model
module_microarchitecture.implementation_style: Synthesizable fallback single-port memory wrapper with the same interface semantics as the macro-compatible SRAM.
module_microarchitecture.control_strategy: Use the same synchronous, chip-selected read/write behavior as the macro-facing memory abstraction, without vendor primitives or exotic inference tricks. Keep the implementation intentionally straightforward so synthesis still recognizes a memory array.
module_microarchitecture.state_strategy: Implement a fixed 64-entry by 32-bit storage array with simple synchronous write behavior and registered or combinational read behavior matching the chosen single-port model intent. No explicit reset should be added inside the memory wrapper.
module_microarchitecture.output_strategy: Drive dout from the modeled memory contents in a way that preserves a memory-like structure visible to synthesis, simulation, and MBIST collateral generation.
ownership_notes: Receives clk, csb, web, addr, and din.
Must remain synthesizable without vendor-only primitives.
Must preserve the same port names and widths as demo_sram_32x64.

sequencing_notes: Reset is synchronous active-low and clears all control/status flops, software-visible read data, BIST latched status, IRQ state, and ready/busy indicators to idle-safe values.
Firmware write cycles update the selected register on wr_en with wr_addr decoding; memory access control is driven only by the MEM_* register fields and not by any hidden protocol.
Firmware read cycles return the addressed register value through rd_data; MEM_RDATA reflects the latest captured memory read data.

Memory writes use MEM_ADDR[5:0] and MEM_WDATA[31:0] with mem_web asserting write-active behavior on the selected SRAM. Memory reads use MEM_ADDR[5:0] with mem_csb asserting access and mem_web deasserted for read.
bist_req is a simple request intent derived from either bist_start or BIST_CONTROL.START. If both are asserted, they are treated equivalently as a BIST request source.
BIST status bits are latched independently of the direct memory access path so that future MBIST collateral can wrap or replace the memory without changing the register contract.
IRQ_STATUS bits are set when done/fail conditions are latched and are cleared by reset, BIST_CONTROL.CLEAR_RESULT, or IRQ_CLEAR write-one-to-clear behavior.
ready should remain a simple availability indicator rather than a complex handshake state machine.
timing_notes: Single clock domain at clk with nominal 50.0 MHz target and 20.0 ns period.
Keep all control and status updates synchronous to clk.
Avoid multi-cycle dependencies or handshakes that would slow simulation or complicate memory inference.
The fallback SRAM model should remain simple enough for standard synthesis tools to infer a 64x32 memory array.
The memory interface is single-port and should be treated as one access per cycle through the selected SRAM abstraction.
consistency_notes: No hierarchy, ports, or filenames may differ from digital_spec_json.
